mn66:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling among the Aṅguttarāpans, at the Aṅguttarāpan market town named Āpaṇa.
mn66:10.1: just as, Udāyī, the king’s elephant, with beam-like tusks, full-grown, noble-bred, accustomed to battle, having been bound with strong ropes as bonds, would merely bend his body a little, snap and tear those bonds apart, and go wherever he wished.
mn66:10.10: And those *bhikkhus* who desire the training, having abandoned it, dwell unconcerned, crest-fallen, living on what is given by others, with a deer-like mind [*cetasā*].
mn66:10.11: For them, Udāyī, that is a weak bond, a feeble bond, a rotten bond, an insubstantial bond.
mn66:10.2: Now if someone were to say, Udāyī:
mn66:10.3: ‘Those bonds by which the king’s elephant, with beam-like tusks, full-grown, noble-bred, accustomed to battle, having been bound with strong ropes as bonds, merely bends his body a little, snaps and tears those bonds apart, and goes wherever he wishes—those for him are a strong bond, a firm bond, a steady bond, a sound bond, a heavy and massive bond’—
mn66:10.6: Those bonds by which the king’s elephant, *bhante*, with beam-like tusks, full-grown, noble-bred, accustomed to battle, having been bound with strong ropes as bonds, merely bends his body a little, snaps and tears those bonds apart, and goes wherever he wishes—those for him are a weak bond, a feeble bond … an insubstantial bond.”
mn66:10.7: “Just so, Udāyī, here some sons of good families, when I say, ‘Give this up,’ say this:
mn66:10.8: ‘Why not give up this trifling, minor thing that ought to be given up, of which the *Bhagavā* speaks of giving up, of which the Fortunate One speaks of relinquishing?’
mn66:10.9: They do give it up, and they do not maintain dissatisfaction with me.
mn66:11.1: Just as, Udāyī, a man might be poor, needy, and not wealthy.
mn66:11.10: those for him are a weak bond, a feeble bond, a rotten bond, an insubstantial bond’—
mn66:11.13: Those bonds by which that man, *bhante*, being bound, is not able, having given up that one little house, dilapidated and falling apart, fit only for crows, nothing much to speak of; having given up that one little cot, dilapidated and falling apart, nothing much to speak of; having given up enough grain to fill one pot, nothing much to speak of; having given up that one wife, nothing much to speak of, to shave off hair and beard, put on ochre robes, and go forth from the home life into homelessness—
mn66:11.14: those for him are a strong bond, a firm bond, a steady bond, a sound bond, a heavy and massive bond.”
mn66:11.15: “Just so, Udāyī, here some foolish men, when I say, ‘Give this up,’ say this:
mn66:11.16: ‘Why is this ascetic so strict over such a trifling, minor matter?’
mn66:11.17: They neither give it up, nor do they abandon their dissatisfaction with me.
mn66:11.18: And for those *bhikkhus* who desire the training, that becomes for them a strong bond, a firm bond, a steady bond, a sound bond, a heavy and massive bond.
mn66:11.2: He might have one little house, dilapidated and falling apart, fit only for crows, nothing much to speak of; one little cot, dilapidated and falling apart, nothing much to speak of; enough grain to fill one pot, nothing much to speak of; and one wife, nothing much to speak of.
mn66:11.3: He might see a *bhikkhu* in a park, with hands and feet well washed, having eaten pleasing food, sitting in the cool shade, devoted to the higher mind [*adhicitte*].
mn66:11.5: ‘How pleasant, good sir, is the contemplative life; how healthy, good sir, is the contemplative life.
mn66:11.6: If only I too could shave off hair and beard, put on ochre robes, and go forth from the home life into homelessness.’
mn66:11.7: But he would not be able, having given up that one little house, dilapidated and falling apart, fit only for crows, nothing much to speak of; having given up that one little cot, dilapidated and falling apart, nothing much to speak of; having given up enough grain to fill one pot, nothing much to speak of; having given up that one wife, nothing much to speak of, to shave off hair and beard, put on ochre robes, and go forth from the home life into homelessness.
mn66:11.8: Now if someone were to say, Udāyī:
mn66:11.9: ‘Those bonds by which that man, being bound, is not able, having given up that one little house, dilapidated and falling apart, fit only for crows, nothing much to speak of; having given up that one little cot, dilapidated and falling apart, nothing much to speak of; having given up enough grain to fill one pot, nothing much to speak of; having given up that one wife, nothing much to speak of, to shave off hair and beard, put on ochre robes, and go forth from the home life into homelessness—
mn66:12.1: Just as, Udāyī, a householder or householder’s son might be rich, affluent, and very wealthy, with stores of many heaps of gold, many heaps of grain, many fields, many estates, many wives, many male slaves, and many female slaves.
mn66:12.11: Those bonds by which that householder or householder’s son, *bhante*, being bound, is able, having given up many heaps of gold, many heaps of grain, many fields, many estates, many wives, many male slaves, and many female slaves, to shave off hair and beard, put on ochre robes, and go forth from the home life into homelessness—
mn66:12.12: those for him are a weak bond, a feeble bond, a rotten bond, an insubstantial bond.”
mn66:12.13: “Just so, Udāyī, here some sons of good families, when I say, ‘Give this up,’ say this:
mn66:12.14: ‘Why not give up this trifling, minor thing that ought to be given up, of which the *Bhagavā* speaks of giving up, of which the Fortunate One speaks of relinquishing?’
mn66:12.15: They do give it up, and they do not maintain dissatisfaction with me.
mn66:12.16: And those *bhikkhus* who desire the training, having abandoned it, dwell unconcerned, crest-fallen, living on what is given by others, with a deer-like mind [*cetasā*].
mn66:12.17: For them, Udāyī, that is a weak bond, a feeble bond, a rotten bond, an insubstantial bond.
mn66:12.2: He might see a *bhikkhu* in a park, with hands and feet well washed, having eaten pleasing food, sitting in the cool shade, devoted to the higher mind [*adhicitte*].
mn66:12.4: ‘How pleasant, good sir, is the contemplative life; how healthy, good sir, is the contemplative life.
mn66:12.5: If only I too could shave off hair and beard, put on ochre robes, and go forth from the home life into homelessness.’
mn66:12.6: He would be able, having given up many heaps of gold, many heaps of grain, many fields, many estates, many wives, many male slaves, and many female slaves, to shave off hair and beard, put on ochre robes, and go forth from the home life into homelessness.
mn66:12.7: Now if someone were to say, Udāyī:
mn66:12.8: ‘Those bonds by which that householder or householder’s son, being bound, is able, having given up many heaps of gold, many heaps of grain, many fields, many estates, many wives, many male slaves, and many female slaves, to shave off hair and beard, put on ochre robes, and go forth from the home life into homelessness—for him those are a strong bond, a firm bond, a steady bond, a sound bond, a heavy and massive bond’—
mn66:13.1: These four kinds of persons, Udāyī, are found existing in the world.
mn66:14.1: Here, Udāyī, one person is practicing for the abandoning of acquisitions, for the relinquishing of acquisitions.
mn66:14.2: As he is practicing for the abandoning of acquisitions, for the relinquishing of acquisitions, thoughts of relish connected with acquisitions arise in him.
mn66:14.3: He tolerates them; he does not give them up, does not dispel them, does not put an end to them, does not bring them to nonexistence.
mn66:14.4: This person, Udāyī, I call ‘bound,’ not ‘unbound.’
mn66:14.6: Because, Udāyī, the diversity of faculties in this person is known to me.
mn66:15.1: But here, Udāyī, one person is practicing for the abandoning of acquisitions, for the relinquishing of acquisitions.
mn66:15.2: As he is practicing for the abandoning of acquisitions, for the relinquishing of acquisitions, thoughts of relish connected with acquisitions arise in him.
mn66:15.3: He does not tolerate them; he gives them up, dispels them, puts an end to them, brings them to nonexistence.
mn66:15.4: This person too, Udāyī, I call ‘bound,’ not ‘unbound.’
mn66:15.6: Because, Udāyī, the diversity of faculties in this person is known to me.
mn66:16.1: But here, Udāyī, one person is practicing for the abandoning of acquisitions, for the relinquishing of acquisitions.
mn66:16.10: But then he quickly gives them up, dispels them, puts an end to them, brings them to nonexistence.
mn66:16.11: This person too, Udāyī, I call ‘bound,’ not ‘unbound.’
mn66:16.13: Because, Udāyī, the diversity of faculties in this person is known to me.
mn66:16.2: As he is practicing for the abandoning of acquisitions, for the relinquishing of acquisitions, from time to time, through a lapse of *sati*, thoughts of relish connected with acquisitions arise in him.
mn66:16.3: Slow, Udāyī, is the arising of *sati*.
mn66:16.4: But then he quickly gives them up, dispels them, puts an end to them, brings them to nonexistence.
mn66:16.5: Just as, Udāyī, if a man were to drop two or three drops of water on an iron pan heated all day,
mn66:16.6: slow, Udāyī, is the falling of the water drops; but they would quickly come to exhaustion and disappearance.
mn66:16.7: Just so, Udāyī, here one person is practicing for the abandoning of acquisitions, for the relinquishing of acquisitions.
mn66:16.8: As he is practicing for the abandoning of acquisitions, for the relinquishing of acquisitions, from time to time, through a lapse of *sati*, thoughts of relish connected with acquisitions arise in him.
mn66:16.9: Slow, Udāyī, is the arising of *sati*.
mn66:17.1: But here, Udāyī, one person, knowing ‘acquisitions are the root of dukkha’—
mn66:17.2: being thus aware, is without acquisitions, liberated through the exhaustion of acquisitions.
mn66:17.3: This person, Udāyī, I call ‘unbound,’ not ‘bound.’
mn66:17.5: Because, Udāyī, the diversity of faculties in this person is known to me.
mn66:17.6: These, Udāyī, are the four kinds of persons found existing in the world.
mn66:18.1: These, Udāyī, are the five cords of sensual pleasure.
mn66:18.3: Forms cognizable by the eye, desirable, lovely, agreeable, appealing, connected with sensuality, enticing;
mn66:18.7: tactile objects cognizable by the body, desirable, lovely, agreeable, appealing, connected with sensuality, enticing.
mn66:18.8: These, Udāyī, are the five cords of sensual pleasure.
mn66:19.1: Whatever pleasure and gladness arises dependent on these five cords of sensual pleasure is called sensual pleasure, filthy pleasure, ordinary-person pleasure, non-ennobled pleasure. It should not be pursued, should not be developed [*bhāvetabbaṁ*], should not be made much of. I say that this pleasure should be feared.
mn66:2.1: Then the *Bhagavā*, in the morning, having dressed and taken robe and bowl, entered Āpaṇa for alms.
mn66:2.2: Having gone for alms in Āpaṇa, after the meal, when he had returned from the almsround, he went to a certain woodland grove for the day’s abiding.
mn66:2.3: Entering that woodland grove, he sat down at the root of a certain tree for the day’s abiding.
mn66:20.1: Here, Udāyī, a *bhikkhu*, quite secluded from sensual pleasures … enters and dwells in the first *jhāna*.
mn66:20.2: With the stilling of thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicāra*] … he enters and dwells in the second *jhāna*,
mn66:20.3: and with the fading away of *pīti* … he enters and dwells in the third *jhāna*,
mn66:20.4: and with the giving up of *sukha* … he enters and dwells in the fourth *jhāna*.
mn66:21.1: This is called the pleasure of renunciation, the pleasure of seclusion [*pavivekasukhaṁ*], the pleasure of stilling, the pleasure of awakening. It should be pursued, developed [*bhāvetabbaṁ*], and made much of. I say that this pleasure is not to be feared.
mn66:22.1: Here, Udāyī, a *bhikkhu*, quite secluded from sensual pleasures … enters and dwells in the first *jhāna*;
mn66:22.2: this, Udāyī, I say is still perturbable.
mn66:22.4: Whatever thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicāra*] there are not yet stilled there: that is what is perturbable there.
mn66:23.1: Here, Udāyī, with the stilling of thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicāra*] … a *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in the second *jhāna*;
mn66:23.2: this too, Udāyī, I say is still perturbable.
mn66:23.4: Whatever *pīti* and *sukha* there remain there not yet stilled: that is what is perturbable there.
mn66:24.1: Here, Udāyī, with the fading away of *pīti* … a *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in the third *jhāna*;
mn66:24.2: this too, Udāyī, I say is still perturbable.
mn66:24.4: Whatever pleasure of equanimity [*upekkhāsukhaṁ*] there remains there not yet stilled: that is what is perturbable there.
mn66:25.1: Here, Udāyī, with the giving up of *sukha* … a *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in the fourth *jhāna*;
mn66:25.2: this, Udāyī, I say is imperturbable.
mn66:26.1: Here, Udāyī, a *bhikkhu*, quite secluded from sensual pleasures … enters and dwells in the first *jhāna*;
mn66:26.2: this, Udāyī, I say is not enough; I say, ‘Give it up’; I say, ‘Go beyond it.’
mn66:26.3: And what is its going beyond?
mn66:27.1: Here, Udāyī, with the stilling of thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicāra*] … a *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in the second *jhāna*: this is its going beyond.
mn66:27.2: This too, Udāyī, I say is not enough; I say, ‘Give it up’; I say, ‘Go beyond it.’
mn66:27.3: And what is its going beyond?
mn66:28.1: Here, Udāyī, with the fading away of *pīti* … a *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in the third *jhāna*: this is its going beyond.
mn66:28.2: This too, Udāyī, I say is not enough; I say, ‘Give it up’; I say, ‘Go beyond it.’
mn66:28.3: And what is its going beyond?
mn66:29.1: Here, Udāyī, with the giving up of *sukha* … a *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in the fourth *jhāna*: this is its going beyond.
mn66:29.2: This too, Udāyī, I say is not enough; I say, ‘Give it up’; I say, ‘Go beyond it.’
mn66:29.3: And what is its going beyond?
mn66:3.1: And Venerable Udāyī too, in the morning, having dressed and taken robe and bowl, entered Āpaṇa for alms.
mn66:3.2: Having gone for alms in Āpaṇa, after the meal, when he had returned from the almsround, he went to that woodland grove for the day’s abiding.
mn66:3.3: Entering that woodland grove, he sat down at the root of a certain tree for the day’s abiding.
mn66:3.4: Then, while Venerable Udāyī was alone in seclusion, this reflection arose in his mind [*cetaso*]:
mn66:30.1: Here, Udāyī, with the complete going beyond of *saññās* of *rūpa* [*rūpasaññānaṁ*], with the vanishing of *saññās* of resistance [*paṭighasaññānaṁ*], not attending to *saññās* of diversity [*nānattasaññānaṁ*], thinking, ‘Infinite space,’ a *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in the dimension of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatanaṁ*]: this is its going beyond.
mn66:30.2: This too, Udāyī, I say is not enough; I say, ‘Give it up’; I say, ‘Go beyond it.’
mn66:30.3: And what is its going beyond?
mn66:31.1: Here, Udāyī, with the complete going beyond of the dimension of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatanaṁ*], thinking, ‘Infinite consciousness [*viññāṇa*],’ a *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in the dimension of infinite consciousness [*viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ*]: this is its going beyond.
mn66:31.2: This too, Udāyī, I say is not enough; I say, ‘Give it up’; I say, ‘Go beyond it.’
mn66:31.3: And what is its going beyond?
mn66:32.1: Here, Udāyī, with the complete going beyond of the dimension of infinite consciousness [*viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ*], thinking, ‘There is nothing,’ a *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ*]: this is its going beyond.
mn66:32.2: This too, Udāyī, I say is not enough; I say, ‘Give it up’; I say, ‘Go beyond it.’
mn66:32.3: And what is its going beyond?
mn66:33.1: Here, Udāyī, with the complete going beyond of the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ*], a *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in the dimension of neither *saññā* nor non-*saññā* [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatanaṁ*]: this is its going beyond.
mn66:33.2: This too, Udāyī, I say is not enough; I say, ‘Give it up’; I say, ‘Go beyond it.’
mn66:33.3: And what is its going beyond?
mn66:34.1: Here, Udāyī, with the complete going beyond of the dimension of neither *saññā* nor non-*saññā* [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatanaṁ*], a *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā* [*saññāvedayitanirodhaṁ*]: this is its going beyond.
mn66:34.2: Thus, Udāyī, I speak even of the giving up of the dimension of neither *saññā* nor non-*saññā* [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatanassapi*].
mn66:34.3: Do you see, Udāyī, any fetter, subtle or gross, of which I do not speak of the giving up?”
mn66:34.6: Satisfied, Venerable Udāyī rejoiced in what the *Bhagavā* had said.
mn66:34.7: The Simile of the Quail is finished, the sixth.
mn66:4.1: “Indeed, the *Bhagavā* has removed many painful things for us, and has brought many pleasant things for us;
mn66:4.2: indeed, the *Bhagavā* has removed many unwholesome *dhammas* for us, and has brought many wholesome *dhammas* for us.”
mn66:5.1: Then, in the evening, Venerable Udāyī rose from seclusion and went to the *Bhagavā*. Having gone to the *Bhagavā*, he bowed to him and sat down to one side. Sitting to one side, Venerable Udāyī said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn66:6.1: “Here, *bhante*, while I was alone in seclusion, this reflection arose in my mind [*cetaso*]:
mn66:6.10: Then, *bhante*, we ate in the evening and in the morning.
mn66:6.11: There was a time, *bhante*, when the *Bhagavā* addressed the *bhikkhus*:
mn66:6.12: ‘Come now, *bhikkhus*, give up this nighttime eating at the wrong time.’
mn66:6.13: Because of that, *bhante*, I was indeed upset, indeed dejected:
mn66:6.14: ‘Even that meal, which is reckoned more exquisite than those two meals—the *Bhagavā* speaks of giving up even that, the Fortunate One speaks of relinquishing even that.’
mn66:6.15: Once, *bhante*, a certain man, having obtained curry by day, said this:
mn66:6.16: ‘Come, set this aside; in the evening we will all eat it together.’
mn66:6.17: Whatever delicacies there are, *bhante*, all those are at night; few are by day.
mn66:6.18: But because we saw love and respect for the *Bhagavā*, and conscience and fear of wrongdoing, we gave up that nighttime eating at the wrong time.
mn66:6.19: In the past, *bhante*, *bhikkhus* going for alms in the dark blackness of night would even walk into cesspits, fall into ditches, climb thorn fences, mount sleeping cows, encounter young men whether engaged in something or not engaged in something, and women would even proposition them in an improper way.
mn66:6.2: ‘Indeed, the *Bhagavā* has removed many painful things for us, and has brought many pleasant things for us;
mn66:6.20: In the past, *bhante*, I too went for alms in the dark blackness of night.
mn66:6.21: A certain woman, *bhante*, saw me by a flash of lightning while she was washing a bowl.
mn66:6.22: Seeing me, terrified, she cried out:
mn66:6.23: ‘It seems a nonhuman goblin has got me!’
mn66:6.24: When she said this, *bhante*, I said to that woman:
mn66:6.26: I am a *bhikkhu*, standing for alms.’
mn66:6.27: ‘A plague on the *bhikkhu*, a plague on the *bhikkhu*’s mother.
mn66:6.28: Better for you, *bhikkhu*, that your belly be slit open with a sharp butcher’s knife than that you wander for alms in the dark blackness of night for the sake of your belly.’
mn66:6.29: When I remember that, *bhante*, this is how it is for me:
mn66:6.3: indeed, the *Bhagavā* has removed many unwholesome *dhammas* for us, and has brought many wholesome *dhammas* for us.’
mn66:6.30: ‘Indeed, the *Bhagavā* has removed many painful things for us, and has brought many pleasant things for us;
mn66:6.31: indeed, the *Bhagavā* has removed many unwholesome *dhammas* for us, and has brought many wholesome *dhammas* for us.’”
mn66:6.4: For formerly, *bhante*, we used to eat in the evening, in the morning, and during the day at the wrong time.
mn66:6.5: There was a time, *bhante*, when the *Bhagavā* addressed the *bhikkhus*:
mn66:6.6: ‘Come now, *bhikkhus*, give up this daytime eating at the wrong time.’
mn66:6.7: Because of that, *bhante*, I was indeed upset, indeed dejected:
mn66:6.8: ‘Even the fine solid and soft foods that faithful householders give us during the day at the wrong time—the *Bhagavā* speaks of giving up even that, the Fortunate One speaks of relinquishing even that.’
mn66:6.9: But because we saw love and respect for the *Bhagavā*, and conscience and fear of wrongdoing, we gave up that daytime eating at the wrong time.
mn66:7.1: “Just so, Udāyī, here some foolish men, when I say, ‘Give this up,’ say this:
mn66:7.2: ‘Why is this ascetic so strict over such a trifling, minor matter?’
mn66:7.3: They neither give it up, nor do they abandon their dissatisfaction with me.
mn66:7.4: And for those *bhikkhus* who desire the training, that becomes for them a strong bond, a firm bond, a steady bond, a sound bond, a heavy and massive bond—
mn66:8.1: just as, Udāyī, if a quail were bound with a rotten creeper, and right there met with slaughter, captivity, or death.
mn66:8.10: And for those *bhikkhus* who desire the training, that becomes for them a strong bond, a firm bond, a steady bond, a sound bond, a heavy and massive bond.
mn66:8.2: Now if someone were to say, Udāyī:
mn66:8.3: ‘That bond by which the quail, bound with a rotten creeper, meets right there with slaughter, captivity, or death—that for her is a weak bond, a feeble bond, a rotten bond, an insubstantial bond’—
mn66:8.6: That bond by which that quail, *bhante*, bound with a rotten creeper, meets right there with slaughter, captivity, or death—that for her is a strong bond, a firm bond, a steady bond, a sound bond, a heavy and massive bond.”
mn66:8.7: “Just so, Udāyī, here some foolish men, when I say, ‘Give this up,’ say this:
mn66:8.8: ‘Why is this ascetic so strict over such a trifling, minor matter?’
mn66:8.9: They neither give it up, nor do they abandon their dissatisfaction with me.
mn66:9.1: But here, Udāyī, some sons of good families, when I say, ‘Give this up,’ say this:
mn66:9.2: ‘Why not give up this trifling, minor thing that ought to be given up, of which the *Bhagavā* speaks of giving up, of which the Fortunate One speaks of relinquishing?’
mn66:9.3: They do give it up, and they do not maintain dissatisfaction with me.
mn66:9.4: And those *bhikkhus* who desire the training, having abandoned it, dwell unconcerned, crest-fallen, living on what is given by others, with a deer-like mind [*cetasā*].
mn66:9.5: For them, Udāyī, that is a weak bond, a feeble bond, a rotten bond, an insubstantial bond—
